figures.caption: Figure 3: Visual evidence of emergent self-correction and strategy adaptation. When an initial plan (top) fails to fully open the drawer, VICX treats the failure as informative visual context. The video generation model then functions as a reasoning engine, adaptively replanning a novel grasping strategy (bottom) to complete the task based on observed environmental feedback.

Figure 9: Representative cross-embodiment case studies under the shifted arm-body and MetaWorld environment condition. Each row shows five frames sampled within a single planning cycle. The failure examples are taken from planning cycles that do not yet reach the target state, while the paired success examples show the corresponding successful planning cycles that recover under the same shifted benchmark. For button-press-topdown, the success case reaches the intended press state after an earlier failed top-down attempt. For drawer-close, the failure case stalls before full closure whereas the success case reaches the final closed state. Together, these paired cycle-level examples illustrate the self-correction behavior enabled by VICX behind the aggregate benchmark reported in Sec. 3.2.

Table 5: Cross-embodiment closed-loop comparison with and without inference context. Both settings use the same nine-task benchmark, ten-cycle budget, and the same arm-body and environment shift. The 5-context setting retrieves five image-state references per query frame, whereas the 0- context setting disables retrieval during inference while keeping the same V2T-ICON checkpoint fixed. PPC denotes the penalized planning count from Appendix B.2; under this evaluation protocol it is equal to the mean completed planning cycles because failed runs consume the full 10-cycle budget.

Figure 10: Retrieval illustration under the cross-embodiment benchmark. The top panels show one generated planning frame and its segmented whole-arm query image under the shifted arm-body and scene condition. The bottom panels show the top retrieved whole-arm references used by V2T-ICON at inference time. This example illustrates that retrieval is driven by arm-pose alignment rather than task identity or scene appearance, which is consistent with the task-agnostic design of the retrieval space.
Figure 6: Overview of representative Meta-World tasks in simulation.

Figure 2: Overview of the Video-to-Trajectory In-Context Operator Network pipeline. (a) Reference retrieval: for each arm-only query frame, we search the arm-only training pool Dtrain and select the top-N visually similar image-state pairs as references. (b) Operator network architecture: query and reference inputs are encoded by image and state encoders, reference attention performs per-frame in-context matching, temporal attention propagates information across frames, and the trajectory decoder predicts X̂ .

Table 4: Closed-loop inference comparison with and without context. The five-context setting uses retrieved image-state references for each query frame, while the no-context setting removes these references from the same closed-loop inference stack. drawer-open is included in the training dataset of V2T-ICON; all other tasks are out-of-distribution tasks.
Figure 7: Full Wan task prompt used for the drawer-open closed-loop evaluation.
Figure 8: Additional examples of emergent self-correction and adaptive strategy results in VICX manipulation on button-press-topdown and faucet-open.

Table 1: Closed-loop simulation success rates on the 9 Meta-World tasks, comparing the π0.5Scratch [3], π0.5-Finetune, AVDC [10], and VICX in the 5-Context setting. Each task is evaluated independently over 20 random seeds. Bold denotes the highest success rate for each task.
Table 3: V2T-ICON architecture and training hyperparameters.
